Party_Details.Name: Rabiah Bee Bte Mohamed Ibrahim

Facts.Text: Rabiah Bee binte Mohamed Ibrahim, also known as Rabiah Weiss, is the elder sister of Salem Ibrahim and was well known as a businesswoman in the fashion industry in Singapore in the 1970s and 1980s.
In 1988, Rabiah sold her fashion business in Singapore and moved to London.
By 1994, Rabiah owned four houses in London and used rental from some of the houses to support herself and her children.
In early 1996, Rabiah bought two more houses in London, bringing her total London houses to six.
In mid 1996, Rabiah and her husband Pierre-Alain Weiss stayed as house guests of her brother Salem Ibrahim in Singapore for several days.
During the mid 1996 stay in Singapore, Rabiah and Salem discussed jointly entering the Greater London property market.
In mid 1996, Rabiah and Salem reached an oral agreement to conduct a venture to buy and refurbish residential properties in Greater London with the intention of letting or selling them at a profit.
Between October 1996 and February 1998, Rabiah arranged for the purchase of eight London properties, at least seven of which were intended for the joint venture.
Rabiah's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that each party would provide capital in equal shares with an initial contribution of £100,000 each.

Rabiah's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that legal title to the joint venture properties would be held by offshore companies and that the beneficial interests would be held for Rabiah and Salem in equal shares as tenants in common.

Rabiah's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that Salem would manage financial and legal aspects of the purchases, including managing jointly provided capital, incorporating the offshore companies, handling their affairs, procuring mortgages, and making mortgage-related payments.
Rabiah's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that she would identify suitable properties, refurbish them, arrange for them to be rented out, and manage them.
Rabiah's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that rental proceeds and profits from resale of the joint venture properties would be shared equally between her and Salem.

Rabiah's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that she would be entitled to reasonable compensation for time, effort, and expense incurred in performing her obligations to the venture.

Rabiah alternatively pleaded that it was an implied term of the venture that she would be entitled to reasonable compensation for her effort and expenditure, including a quantum meruit calculated at 15% of gross rental proceeds of the joint venture properties.

Rabiah pleaded that it was an implied term of the venture that both parties would account to each other for all dealings and property and assets coming into their hands or under their control in the course of the venture, and that a joint venture account would be opened and operated for this purpose.
In late August 1996, Rabiah remitted £100,000 as capital contribution to a personal bank account in Jersey held jointly by Salem and his wife, following his instructions.
On 25 October 1996, 57 Silvester Road was purchased in the name of Untong Properties Ltd as a joint venture property.
On 6 November 1996, 22 St Aidans Road was purchased in the name of Senang Properties Ltd as a joint venture property.
On 15 November 1996, 79 Mundania Road was purchased in the name of Anak Properties Ltd as a joint venture property.
On 25 November 1996, 48 Hansler Road was purchased in the name of Bintang Properties Ltd as a joint venture property.
On 8 January 1997, 49 Peckham Rye was purchased in the name of Tanah Merah Properties Ltd as a joint venture property.
On 7 March 1997, 1 Upland Road was purchased in the name of Mutiara Properties Ltd as a joint venture property.
On 19 February 1998, 24 Goodrich Road was purchased in the name of Greenholme Assets Ltd as a joint venture property.
On 8 April 1998, 71A Glengarry Road was purchased and registered under Elsenham Consultant Ltd, a British Virgin Islands company.

Rabiah held powers of attorney from each of the joint venture companies authorising her to act for them in matters relating to their respective properties, including management, buying, selling, and creating interests.
Rabiah instructed Mapletoft, through solicitor Donal O'Sullivan, to act in the purchase and mortgage of the first six joint venture properties.
Rabiah corresponded directly with Mapletoft regarding the purchase and mortgage of the joint venture properties.
On 12 December 1996, Rabiah and Salem signed and accepted facility letters from Lloyds Bank in respect of the first six joint venture companies.

On 15 May 1997, Lloyds disbursed £309,400 under loan facilities for the first six joint venture properties, of which £300,000 was paid into Salem's sterling accounts and £146,100 remained in blocked interest-earning deposit accounts at Lloyds; £54,500 remained undrawn.
Facility letters for Goodrich and Glengarry were executed after their purchases, and both properties were mortgaged to Lloyds Bank.
In 1997, Rabiah incorporated Pertama Properties Limited in the United Kingdom to collect rent earned by the joint venture properties.
From 1997 onwards, rental income from the joint venture properties, except Glengarry, was collected and at various times paid into accounts controlled by Rabiah or Pertama.
In 1998, Rabiah and Salem decided to sell 22 St Aidans Road and 79 Mundania Road.
In early 1999, the sales of St Aidans and Mundania were completed, and the mortgages on these two properties were redeemed and the related loans repaid.
Following the sales of St Aidans and Mundania, the net sale proceeds were remitted to accounts specified by Salem, and Rabiah did not contemporaneously request distribution of her share.
On 10 September 2000, Salem gave notice of redemption to Lloyds Bank in relation to outstanding loans, following discussions with Rabiah about closing venture accounts and redeeming guarantees.
By late 2000, Rabiah raised concerns to Salem about accounting for venture moneys and funds in his personal accounts.

On 3 August 2001, Rabiah and Salem signed a written settlement agreement, brokered by their sibling Victor Adam Ibrahim, to dissolve the venture, retain their respective capital contributions, and split remaining proceeds equally.

Under the 3 August 2001 settlement agreement, Rabiah and Salem agreed that each would be refunded capital contributions, any excess over 50% would be returned with interest, each would account for moneys and interest held, and unsold properties would be valued with Rabiah having a right of first refusal at 50% of valuation.

Under the 3 August 2001 settlement agreement, Rabiah agreed to pay £121,000 to retire an outstanding Lloyds Bank loan, and Salem agreed to procure Lloyds to release charges over unsold properties, deliver title deeds to Victor Adam Ibrahim as stakeholder, and secure Rabiah's release from personal guarantees.
On 13 August 2001, arrangements were made to redeem mortgages to Lloyds on certain joint venture properties, and Salem remitted his share of the redemption amount to Lloyds.

On 16 August 2001, Lloyds Bank wrote to Rabiah and Salem confirming that they had been discharged from liability as guarantors for facilities in respect of five properties and setting out conditions for discharge regarding remaining properties.
On 18 October 2000, Salem wrote to Lloyds Bank authorising Rabiah to inspect, view, and obtain disclosure of all documentation related to the joint venture properties.
On 28 August 2001, Salem wrote again to Lloyds Bank authorising Rabiah to obtain information and documents related to the joint venture properties.
In March or April 2003, Rabiah received an account from Salem prepared by Gane Shawn & Partners and allegedly verified by Duncan Merrin, without supporting documents.
In August 2001, Rabiah corresponded directly with Lloyds Bank regarding the joint venture loans and facilities.
In September 2002, after termination of the venture, Glengarry was first let out and began earning rental income.

In March or April 2003, Rabiah received an account of the venture from Salem that had been prepared by Gane Shawn & Partners and reviewed by chartered accountant Duncan Merrin, without supporting documents.
From late 2000 to early 2003, Rabiah repeatedly requested accounts from Salem regarding venture funds, properties, and companies.
By 2001, Rabiah had lodged cautions against the joint venture properties based on her 50% shareholding in the joint venture companies and payment of 50% of purchase prices.
On 12 November 2003, Rabiah commenced Suit 1079/2003 seeking an account of the venture and remuneration for her work in renting out and maintaining the joint venture properties.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, on the pre-dispute facts, the commercial arrangement between Rabiah and Salem over the London properties and offshore companies, including capital contributions, property acquisitions, loan facilities, rental collection, and the 3 August 2001 settlement agreement, resulted in Salem being provide further payment or accounting to Rabiah beyond what was already provided, and whether Glengarry and specific sale proceeds and rentals should be included in any such accounting.
Party_Details.Rule: In joint commercial ventures involving property acquisitions through corporate vehicles, parties' rights to payment and accounting are generally determined by the agreed terms of their arrangement, including any subsequent settlement agreements, the documented flow of funds, and the allocation of ownership and profits, as evidenced by contracts, banking records, and corporate documentation.
Party_Details.Application: Rabiah contributed capital and arranged property purchases while Salem managed loan facilities and used his personal accounts for venture funds, with both parties using their own accounts to hold venture-related moneys. Properties were acquired through offshore companies with equal ownership, and rental income was collected primarily through accounts controlled by Rabiah or Pertama. The parties later signed a written settlement agreement on 3 August 2001 specifying how capital, loans, and unsold properties were to be dealt with, including a specified payment to Lloyds and mutual accounting obligations. Subsequent banking documentation, authorisations to Lloyds, and the court-appointed reconstruction of accounts provide a record of loan disbursements, repayments, and sale proceeds, including the treatment of St Aidans, Mundania, and other properties. These pre-dispute events frame whether any additional sums or documentation remained outstanding from Salem to Rabiah under their arrangement and the settlement terms.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment analyses whether a solicitor-client relationship existed between the siblings, concluding that Salem did not act as Rabiah's personal solicitor in the venture, and that there was no express or implied retainer. It then determines that their arrangement amounted to a partnership under Singapore law, based on the ongoing nature of the property business, shared profits, and active roles, despite the use of offshore companies. The court rejects the claim that Salem owed fiduciary duties to Rabiah in his capacity as director of the offshore companies, applying the principle that directors owe duties to the company, not directly to shareholders, and noting that the action was not brought as a derivative claim. On the bank account issue, the court finds there was no agreement to maintain a separate joint venture account, that both parties mixed venture funds with personal funds, and that Rabiah's complaint about non-segregation arose only after the relationship broke down. The court accepts that Salem used venture funds for personal expenses without informing Rabiah but finds no demonstrated financial loss because the venture was profitable and sufficient funds remained to satisfy entitlements. Regarding record-keeping, the court notes that both parties had obligations to keep records, that many documents were in Rabiah's possession via Mapletoft and powers of attorney, and that Salem authorised Lloyds to disclose documents to her. The court finds no loss arising from any deficiencies in Salem's record-keeping, especially given Lloyds' confirmation that all facilities were repaid and no undisclosed advances existed. On Peckham Rye, the court holds that Salem had no control over the mortgagee's sale, that the sale was conducted by a receiver and auctioneers, and that the achieved price was within or above valuation, with no evidence of a higher obtainable price. On St Aidans and Mundania, the court finds no agreed requirement for immediate distribution of sale proceeds, notes Rabiah's lack of contemporaneous written demand for her share and her own practice of retaining rental income without distribution, and concludes there was no breach by Salem in retaining proceeds for loan-related purposes. The court considers the settlement agreement defence, rejecting unpleaded grounds of duress and undue influence and the argument based on Salem's role as director. It interprets clause 13 and assesses whether Salem's obligations under that clause were conditions precedent to Rabiah's payment obligation, and evaluates performance of those obligations, including redemption of loans and release of guarantees. The judgment also addresses whether Glengarry was part of the venture, whether Rabiah is entitled to remuneration for her management work, the adequacy of accounts provided by both parties, and the weight to be given to the expert accounting reports. Ultimately, the court reaches conclusions on liability, the scope of any accounting, the status of Glengarry, and the effect of the settlement agreement on the parties' claims and counterclaims, and allocates costs accordingly.

Party_Details.Name: Salem Ibrahim

Facts.Text: Salem Ibrahim started practice as a lawyer in Singapore in 1989 after previously working in the insurance industry.
By 1996, Salem was a partner in the law firm Harry Elias & Partners.
At the end of May 1997, Salem left Harry Elias & Partners to establish his own legal firm, which he headed thereafter.
In mid 1996, Salem hosted his sister Rabiah and her husband as house guests in Singapore, during which they discussed a joint property venture in Greater London.
In mid 1996, Salem and Rabiah concluded an oral agreement to conduct a venture to buy and refurbish residential properties in Greater London for letting or sale at a profit.

Salem's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that the parties would purchase up to 20 properties and would from time to time contribute equally to 20% of the purchase price of such properties, without a fixed initial capital of £100,000 each.

Salem's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that his personal sterling bank accounts would be used by the parties for tax efficiency and that properties would be held in the names of offshore companies in which each party would have equal shareholding.

Salem's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that he would arrange financing for each joint venture company and property, obtain mortgage facilities using his income statements, and make payments from his personal bank accounts for property purchases and mortgage payments.

Salem's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that decisions to purchase properties and management of each purchase would be taken jointly in consultation, that Rabiah would identify and refurbish properties and collect rent into her own account, and that both parties would share equally in profits and rental through the joint venture companies.
Salem's pleaded version of the oral agreement terms included that the parties would not compete with the venture in the Fulham and Dulwich areas.
In late August 1996, Salem instructed Rabiah to remit her capital contribution to his joint personal bank account in Jersey, which he held with his wife, and he received £100,000 into that account.
Salem arranged for the incorporation of offshore joint venture companies in the Seychelles and the British Virgin Islands to hold legal title to each property acquired for the venture.
Salem was the sole director of the joint venture companies that held the London properties.
Salem coordinated with Mapletoft, through Donal O'Sullivan, for the purchase and mortgage of the first six joint venture properties, while Mapletoft acted as English solicitors.
On 12 December 1996, Salem and Rabiah signed and accepted Lloyds Bank facility letters for loan financing of the first six joint venture properties.

On 15 May 1997, Lloyds Bank disbursed £309,400 under loan facilities for the first six joint venture properties, of which £300,000 was credited to Salem's sterling accounts, with £146,100 retained in blocked deposit accounts and £54,500 undrawn.
Salem used his personal bank accounts to make payments for property purchases and to service mortgage obligations for the joint venture properties.
Salem arranged for M/s Harry Elias & Partners to procure incorporation and annual licence renewals of the joint venture companies and charged incorporation-related costs to the venture.
In 1998, Salem and Rabiah decided to sell 22 St Aidans Road and 79 Mundania Road, and Salem coordinated the handling of the sale proceeds.
In early 1999, after the sales of St Aidans and Mundania, the net sale proceeds were remitted to accounts under Salem's control, and he retained the proceeds without immediate distribution to Rabiah.
On 10 September 2000, Salem issued a notice of redemption to Lloyds Bank in relation to outstanding loans following discussions with Rabiah about closing venture accounts and redeeming guarantees.
On 18 October 2000, Salem wrote to Lloyds Bank authorising Rabiah to inspect and obtain disclosure of all documentation related to the joint venture properties.
Around February 2001, Salem sent Rabiah various documents, including bank-related documents, concerning the venture and loans.

On 3 August 2001, Salem signed a written settlement agreement with Rabiah, brokered by their brother Victor Adam Ibrahim, to dissolve the venture and regulate capital refunds, loan repayments, and division of unsold properties and proceeds.

Under the 3 August 2001 settlement agreement, Salem and Rabiah agreed to refund capital contributions, return any excess over 50% with interest, account for moneys and interest held, deduct interest, costs, and expenses from moneys held, use remaining funds to settle bank loans to equalise cash in hand, value unsold properties, and give Rabiah a right of first refusal to purchase unsold properties at 50% of valuation.

Clause 13 of the 3 August 2001 settlement agreement recorded that Rabiah agreed to immediately pay £121,000 to retire an outstanding Lloyds Bank loan on condition that Salem would procure Lloyds to release charges over unsold properties, deliver title deeds to Victor Adam Ibrahim as stakeholder, and release Rabiah from liabilities under her personal guarantees and other liabilities to Lloyds.
On 13 August 2001, Salem arranged for redemption of mortgages to Lloyds on certain joint venture properties and remitted £6,081.81 as his share of the redemption amount to Lloyds.

On 16 August 2001, Lloyds Bank wrote to Salem and Rabiah confirming that they had been discharged from liability as guarantors for facilities in respect of five properties and setting out that discharge for remaining properties would follow upon redemption of the loans.
On 28 August 2001, Salem wrote again to Lloyds Bank authorising Rabiah to inspect and obtain disclosure of all documentation related to the joint venture properties.
In August 2001, Salem and Rabiah, together with Lloyds representatives, attended a meeting at Lloyds to discuss the joint venture loans and related documentation.

During the venture period, Salem used his personal and joint accounts, including accounts shared with his wife, to receive venture-related funds such as loan disbursements and capital contributions, and to make payments related to the properties and loans.
During the venture period, Salem did not open a separate bank account solely designated as a joint venture account for the venture funds.
Salem retained certain venture funds in his personal accounts and, on some occasions, used funds held for the venture to meet his personal expenses.
In March or April 2003, Salem provided Rabiah with an account of the venture prepared by Gane Shawn & Partners, which had been reviewed by chartered accountant Duncan Merrin.
In September 2004, Salem applied to court for the appointment of an independent expert, resulting in the appointment of Tam Chee Chong of Deloitte & Touche to reconstruct the venture’s accounts.

On 7 January 2005, the court-appointed expert issued a report on the reconstructed accounts of the venture, followed by an addendum on 8 July 2005, which traced loan disbursements, repayments, rental flows, and sale proceeds.

On 18 September 2002, 49 Peckham Rye, owned by Tanah Merah Properties Ltd and mortgaged to Lloyds, was sold at a public auction conducted by Andrews Robertson for £260,000 under a mortgagee sale initiated by Lloyds.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on the pre-dispute conduct and agreements, including Salem's use of personal accounts for venture funds, his management of loan facilities and property companies, and the 3 August 2001 settlement agreement, Salem is make further payments or provide further accounting to Rabiah, and whether Glengarry and specific sale proceeds and rentals fall within any such obligations.
Party_Details.Rule: In disputes over joint commercial ventures structured through companies, a participant's financial and accounting obligations to another participant are generally determined by the agreed terms of their venture and any subsequent settlement agreement, interpreted in light of banking records, corporate roles, and the documented flow of funds, including how sale proceeds, loan redemptions, and rental income were handled.
Party_Details.Application: Salem and Rabiah agreed orally to a property investment venture using offshore companies, with Salem handling financing and using his personal accounts for tax efficiency, and Rabiah managing property identification, refurbishment, and rental collection. Loan facilities from Lloyds were documented and disbursed largely into Salem's accounts, while rental income was collected mainly through accounts controlled by Rabiah or Pertama. Both parties used mixed accounts for venture and personal funds. They later executed a written settlement agreement on 3 August 2001 that specified how capital, loans, and unsold properties were to be treated, including a defined payment by Rabiah to Lloyds and mutual accounting obligations. Salem arranged loan redemptions and obtained bank confirmations regarding guarantees, and he later provided an account prepared by Gane Shawn & Partners and supported an independent reconstruction by a court-appointed expert. These pre-dispute steps and records frame whether any additional sums or documentation remained outstanding from Salem to Rabiah under their arrangement and the settlement terms, and whether properties such as Glengarry and proceeds from St Aidans, Mundania, and Peckham Rye are to be included in any further accounting.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court evaluates Salem's contention that the venture was a contractual joint venture rather than a partnership and that he was not Rabiah's solicitor, ultimately finding that no solicitor-client relationship existed but that the arrangement amounted to a partnership under the Partnership Act, given the ongoing nature of the business, shared profits, and active roles. It rejects the argument that Salem owed fiduciary duties to Rabiah as a shareholder based on his role as sole director of the offshore companies, applying Singapore company law and the proper plaintiff rule. The court examines Salem's use of personal and joint accounts for venture funds, noting that both parties mixed venture and personal funds and that there was no agreed requirement for a separate joint venture account. It acknowledges that Salem used venture funds for personal expenses without informing Rabiah but finds no demonstrated financial loss because the venture was profitable and sufficient funds remained to satisfy entitlements. On record-keeping, the court finds that while Salem's records were not ideal, Rabiah had access to many documents through Mapletoft and powers of attorney, and Salem authorised Lloyds to disclose documents to her; no loss is shown from any deficiencies. Regarding Peckham Rye, the court finds that the property was sold by Lloyds as mortgagee through a receiver and auctioneer, that Salem had no control over the sale process, and that the sale price was within or above valuation, with no evidence of a higher achievable price. On St Aidans and Mundania, the court concludes that there was no agreed requirement for immediate distribution of sale proceeds, that Salem was entitled to retain proceeds for loan-related purposes, and that Rabiah's lack of contemporaneous written demand and her own retention of rental income support this view. The court considers Salem's reliance on the 3 August 2001 settlement agreement as a defence, rejecting unpleaded grounds of duress and undue influence and interpreting clause 13 to determine whether Salem's obligations to procure releases and deliver title deeds were conditions precedent to Rabiah's payment obligation. It assesses Salem's performance under the settlement, including arranging loan redemptions and obtaining bank confirmations, and evaluates whether the settlement superseded earlier accounting obligations. The judgment also addresses whether Glengarry formed part of the venture, whether Rabiah is entitled to remuneration for her management work, the adequacy of accounts provided by both parties, and the weight to be given to the expert accounting reports. Based on these analyses, the court reaches conclusions on Salem's liability, the scope of any accounting or payments due, the status of Glengarry, the effect of the settlement agreement, and the allocation of costs.
